perturbation.explanation: Moving the fee and payment schedule information into the client acknowledgement section will reduce its enforceability by creating a section where it would not be normally found.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Truth in Lending Act (TILA) - Specifically, the disclosure requirements for payment schedules.
perturbation.law_explanation: TILA requires clear and conspicuous disclosure of payment schedules. Placing this information within an 'Acknowledgement' section buries the information and obscures the clarity of the fee payment schedule, which impedes the client's ability to easily understand the payment responsibilities as required by TILA. TILA requires this information be prominent and easily understandable.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 15 U.S. Code § 1638 - Transactions other than under an open end credit plan | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 15 U.S. Code § 1638 - Transactions other than under an open end credit plan U.S. Code Notes Authorities (CFR) prev | next (a) Required disclosures by creditor For each consumer credit transaction other than under an open end credit plan, the creditor shall disclose each of the following items, to the extent applicable: (1) The identity of the creditor required to make disclosure. (2) (A) The “amount financed”, using that term, which shall be the amount of credit of which the consumer has actual use. This amount shall be computed as follows, but the computations need not be disclosed and shall not be disclosed with the disclosures conspicuously segregated in accordance with subsection (b)(1): (i) take the principal amount of the loan or the cash price less downpayment and trade-in; (ii) add any charges which are not part of the finance charge or of the principal amount of the loan and which are financed by the consumer, including the cost of any items excluded from the finance charge pursuant to section 1605 of this title ; and (iii) subtract any charges which are part of the finance charge but which will be paid by the consumer before or at the time of the consummation of the transaction, or have been withheld from the proceeds of the credit. (B) In conjunction with the disclosure of the amount financed, a creditor shall provide a statement of the consumer’s right to obtain, upon a written request, a written itemization of the amount financed . The statement shall include spaces for a “yes” and “no” indication to be initialed by the consumer to indicate whether the consumer wants a written itemization of the amount financed . Upon receiving an affirmative indication, the creditor shall provide, at the time other disclosures are required to be furnished, a written
perturbation.justification: The change contradicts TILA (as explained by the LLM) because moving the fee and payment schedule into the 'Acknowledgement' section buries the information, obscuring the clarity of the fee payment schedule. This impedes the client's ability to easily understand the payment responsibilities as required by TILA. This relates to 15 USC 1638 which generally requires disclosures by creditors. The Perturbation explanation clearly states that this repositioning will reduce its enforceability. This aligns with the definition of 'Structural Flaws - Legal Contradiction' because a required disclosure is placed in a misleading section.

perturbation.original_text: 5. Statement of Work Website (Customer Portal) Users will be able to go to the Pelican Delivers front-end website and easily search, browse, and order cannabis. Before a user can gain access to the website, they must first verify their birthday (users must be 21 and over to even view the website).
perturbation.changed_text: 7. Acknowledgement by Client Client agrees to the terms of the Statement of work Appendix A hereto attached and acknowledges receipt of a copy of this Agreement. Website (Customer Portal) Users will be able to go to the Pelican Delivers front-end website and easily search, browse, and order cannabis. Before a user can gain access to the website, they must first verify their birthday (users must be 21 and over to even view the website).
perturbation.explanation: Moving the age verification information into the client acknowledgement section will reduce its enforceability by creating a section where it would not be normally found. The age verification requirements are now hidden within a clause about agreement acknowledgement.
perturbation.law_explanation: 27 CFR § 478.99 requires proper identification and verification when selling regulated goods like cannabis, especially regarding age restrictions. Moving the age verification requirement to a general agreement acknowledgement clause undermines the visibility and enforceability of this verification process. This obfuscation hinders the clear understanding and implementation of age verification, potentially leading to violations.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 27 CFR § 478.99 - Certain prohibited sales, purchases, or deliveries. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 27 CFR § 478.99 - Certain prohibited sales, purchases, or deliveries. CFR prev | next § 478.99 Certain prohibited sales, purchases, or deliveries. (a) Interstate sales or deliveries. A licensed importer, licensed manufacturer, licensed dealer, or licensed collector shall not sell or deliver any firearm to any person not licensed under this part and who the licensee knows or has reasonable cause to believe does not reside in (or if a corporation or other business entity, does not maintain a place of business in) the State in which the licensee's place of business or activity is located: Provided, That the foregoing provisions of this paragraph (1) shall not apply to the sale or delivery of a rifle or shotgun (curio or relic, in the case of a licensed collector) to a resident of a State other than the State in which the licensee's place of business or collection premises is located if the requirements of § 478.96(c) are fully met, and (2) shall not apply to the loan or rental of a firearm to any person for temporary use for lawful sporting purposes (see § 478.97 ). (b) Sales or deliveries to underaged persons. A licensed importer, licensed manufacturer, licensed dealer, or licensed collector shall not sell or deliver (1) any firearm or ammunition to any individual who the importer, manufacturer, dealer, or collector knows or has reasonable cause to believe is less than 18 years of age, and, if the firearm, or ammunition, is other than a shotgun or rifle, or ammunition for a shotgun or rifle, to any individual who the importer, manufacturer, dealer, or collector knows or has reasonable cause to believe is less than 21 years of age, or (2) any firearm to any person in any State where the purchase or possession by such person of such firearm would be in violation of any State l
perturbation.justification: The modified text creates a legal contradiction by placing the age verification requirement in an unusual section of the contract (acknowledgement by the client) rather than in the section outlining website user access, which obscures its importance and enforceability. The original text clearly and directly states the age verification requirement for website access. The change moves this requirement into a client acknowledgement section, weakening its prominence. 27 CFR § 478.99 addresses prohibited sales to underaged persons. While the snippets don't explicitly state where age verification must be placed in a contract, placing it in an acknowledgement section undermines its visibility and enforcement. This aligns with the 'Structural Flaws Legal Contradiction' definition, where a required legal disclosure is placed in an irrelevant section, making it difficult to locate or interpret.
